learning_objectives: Map the structure of the modern game industry from indie studios to AAA publishers
Identify the major career paths and roles within game development
Understand how games move from concept to release across different studio sizes
readings: Blood, Sweat, and Pixels, Ch. 1-2 (Jason Schreier)
IGDA Developer Satisfaction Survey, latest edition

lecture_notes.introduction: The game industry generates over $180 billion annually and employs hundreds of thousands worldwide. Understanding its structure, culture, and career paths is essential for entering it prepared.
key_concepts: Industry segments: AAA, AA, indie, mobile, serious games, esports
Publisher-developer relationship models and funding structures
Key roles: design, programming, art, audio, production, QA, marketing
Studio organizational structures and reporting hierarchies
Regional industry hubs and remote work trends
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive studio org chart exercise mapping roles at a hypothetical 50-person studio, identifying dependencies and communication channels
setup_requirements: LinkedIn account for professional networking exercises
Access to GDC Vault (free tier) for postmortem talks

review.value: AAA studios run 2-5 year development cycles with 100+ person teams and publisher funding, while indie studios operate with small teams, shorter cycles, and self-funding or grants
The most employable game developers have deep expertise in one discipline plus broad working knowledge across adjacent disciplines

learning_objectives: Construct a portfolio that demonstrates relevant skills for target roles
Curate and present project work to maximize impact with hiring managers
Build a professional online presence across appropriate platforms
readings: Game Industry Portfolio Best Practices guide (provided on LMS)
Selected portfolio reviews from game industry hiring managers (links on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Your portfolio is the single most important asset in your job search. It must demonstrate not just what you made, but your role, your process, and your problem-solving ability.
key_concepts: Portfolio structure: hero pieces, project case studies, about/contact
Discipline-specific portfolio requirements (design, art, programming, production)
Case study format: problem, process, solution, outcome, reflection
Platform options: personal website, ArtStation, GitHub, itch.io
Common portfolio mistakes: too many pieces, no context, broken links, outdated work
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live review of three game industry portfolios (one strong, one average, one weak) with group critique identifying what works and what does not
setup_requirements: Screenshots and documentation from at least two completed projects
Web hosting account or portfolio platform account

review.value: Hiring managers want to see process documentation, iteration, and problem-solving alongside finished results
Five strong pieces with context outperform twenty mediocre pieces every time; your weakest piece defines your perceived skill level

learning_objectives: Write a game-industry-targeted resume that passes applicant tracking systems and engages human reviewers
Prepare for common interview formats: phone screens, portfolio reviews, design tests, and whiteboard exercises
Develop responses to behavioral and technical interview questions
readings: Ask a Game Dev blog, interview preparation series (provided on LMS)
Gamasutra, 'What We Look For When Hiring' collection

lecture_notes.introduction: Getting hired in the game industry requires navigating multiple interview stages. Each stage tests different skills, and preparation is the strongest predictor of success.
key_concepts: Resume format: one page, accomplishment-driven bullet points, quantified results
ATS optimization: keywords from job postings, standard section headers, clean formatting
Interview types: behavioral (STAR method), technical (live coding, system design), portfolio walkthrough
Design test strategies: scope management, documentation, time boxing
Salary negotiation fundamentals and industry compensation data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mock interview session: instructor conducts a live portfolio review interview with a volunteer, followed by group debrief on technique
setup_requirements: Current resume draft for in-class workshop
One project ready for a 5-minute portfolio walkthrough

review.value: Situation, Task, Action, Result: a structured framework for answering behavioral interview questions with specific, measurable examples
Studios evaluate design test submissions on clarity of thought and documentation quality, not on how much content you cram in; scoping well is part of the test

class_schedule.topic: Project Management: Agile and Scrum in Game Development
learning_objectives: Apply Agile and Scrum methodologies to game development workflows
Manage a product backlog and plan sprints for a game project
Identify how game studios adapt standard Agile practices to creative production
readings: Agile Game Development with Scrum, Ch. 1-4 (Clinton Keith)
GDC Talk: 'Agile at Riot Games' postmortem (GDC Vault)

lecture_notes.introduction: Most game studios use some form of Agile methodology, but pure Scrum rarely survives contact with creative production. Understanding both the framework and how studios adapt it is essential.
key_concepts: Scrum roles in game dev: product owner (creative director), scrum master, development team
Sprint planning for creative work: accounting for iteration and discovery
User stories for games: player-facing vs. technical stories
Estimation techniques: story points, planning poker, reference sizing
Retrospectives and continuous improvement in studio culture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Sprint planning exercise: teams break down a hypothetical game feature into user stories, estimate effort, and plan a two-week sprint using a shared Trello/Jira board
setup_requirements: Familiarity with basic project management concepts

review.value: Sprints are short fixed-length iterations (1-4 weeks) with adjustable scope, while milestones are fixed-scope targets (alpha, beta, gold) with adjustable timelines
A time-boxed exploration task used in game Agile to investigate unknowns (art style tests, mechanic prototypes) before committing to full production

learning_objectives: Distinguish between copyright, trademark, patent, and trade secret protections as applied to games
Identify key clauses in employment and contractor agreements that affect ownership of work
Recognize when to seek legal counsel for IP and contract issues
readings: The Game Developer's Guide to IP Law (selected chapters, provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding intellectual property law protects both your creative work and your career. Every game developer will encounter IP issues, from hiring contracts to asset licensing to publishing agreements.
key_concepts: Copyright: automatic protection for creative expression, not ideas or mechanics
Trademark: protection for names, logos, and distinctive identifiers
Patents in games: controversial but real (e.g., Namco's loading screen mini-game patent)
Work-for-hire doctrine: who owns what you create as an employee vs. contractor
Key contract clauses: assignment of IP, non-compete, non-disclosure, royalty structures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Contract clause analysis exercise: review a sample game developer employment agreement and identify clauses that affect your rights, with instructor commentary on negotiation points
setup_requirements: Sample contracts provided on LMS (anonymized real agreements)

review.value: Under US copyright law, work created by an employee within the scope of employment belongs to the employer; contractors retain ownership unless a written agreement assigns it
Copyright protects the specific expression (code, art, audio, text) but not the underlying rules or mechanics, which is why genre clones are legal but asset theft is not

learning_objectives: Analyze the systemic causes of crunch culture in the game industry
Evaluate studio practices and policies that promote sustainable development
Develop personal strategies for maintaining work-life balance in high-pressure creative environments
readings: Schreier, 'The Horrible World of Video Game Crunch' (Kotaku long-form)
IGDA Quality of Life White Paper, latest edition

lecture_notes.introduction: Crunch is the game industry's most persistent labor issue. Understanding why it happens, who it harms, and what alternatives exist is not optional knowledge for any industry professional.
key_concepts: Defining crunch: sustained overtime beyond standard working hours, often unpaid or culturally coerced
Root causes: scope creep, poor estimation, hero culture, misaligned incentives
Health impacts: burnout, depression, relationship strain, reduced productivity after 50 hours/week
Studio models that avoid crunch: Supergiant Games, Media Molecule, Respawn (pre-acquisition)
Personal sustainability: boundary setting, career planning, knowing when to leave
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study discussion: Rockstar Games' Red Dead Redemption 2 crunch controversy, examining the timeline, studio response, and industry-wide impact on labor conversations
setup_requirements: Pre-reading of assigned Schreier article completed before class

review.value: Research consistently shows that sustained work weeks beyond 50 hours produce diminishing returns, with output per hour declining and error rates increasing after the second consecutive week of overtime
The most effective crunch prevention is disciplined scope management during pre-production, making it a production leadership responsibility rather than an individual developer problem

questions.question: A junior game designer is preparing their portfolio for applications to both AAA studios and indie teams. Describe three specific ways the portfolio should differ between these two targets, and explain why each difference matters to the respective hiring managers.
questions.answer: 1) Scope of work shown: AAA portfolios should highlight your specific contribution within large teams with clear role delineation, while indie portfolios should demonstrate breadth and ability to wear multiple hats. AAA hiring managers need to see you can execute within a specialized pipeline; indie managers need to know you can ship a game with minimal support. 2) Documentation depth: AAA portfolios should include detailed design documentation (GDDs, system specs, balancing spreadsheets) showing you can communicate in structured formats, while indie portfolios should emphasize rapid prototyping and iteration evidence. 3) Tool proficiency: AAA portfolios should demonstrate proficiency with industry-standard tools (Unreal, Jira, Perforce), while indie portfolios should show versatility with accessible tools and the ability to build custom solutions. The tools signal whether you can integrate into an existing pipeline vs. build one.

rubric.key: Clear reasoning connecting each difference to hiring context
Demonstrates understanding of AAA vs. indie workflow differences

questions.question: Write a resume bullet point for the following scenario using the accomplishment-driven format: You were the lead programmer on a student game project with 4 team members, built in Unity over 12 weeks, that won Best Gameplay at your school's annual game showcase.
questions.answer: Led gameplay programming for a 4-person team over 12 weeks in Unity, architecting core combat and AI systems that earned Best Gameplay recognition at the annual showcase, judged by industry professionals from studios including [Studio Name].

rubric.key: Uses active verb and specifies leadership role
Includes quantifiable details (team size, duration, or scope)
Specifies technical contribution, not just management

questions.question: Your game studio is three weeks from a milestone deadline. The team estimates 5 weeks of work remaining in the backlog. As the producer, describe the specific steps you would take using Agile methodology to address this situation, including how you would communicate with stakeholders.
questions.answer: 1) Call an emergency backlog triage with the team to categorize all remaining items as must-have, should-have, and nice-to-have for the milestone. 2) Identify items that can be descoped or deferred to the next sprint cycle without compromising the milestone's core deliverable. 3) Look for tasks that can be parallelized or simplified by reducing scope (e.g., placeholder art instead of final assets for non-critical elements). 4) Communicate transparently with stakeholders: present the gap, the triage results, and two options (reduced scope on time, or full scope with timeline extension), with a recommendation. 5) If scope reduction is chosen, update the backlog and sprint plan, then hold daily standups to track progress against the revised plan with no further scope additions.

rubric.key: Identifies backlog triage and prioritization as first step
Includes stakeholder communication with options and recommendation
Proposes ongoing tracking mechanism after the decision

questions.question: A game developer leaves Studio A to join Studio B. At Studio A, they worked on a custom procedural terrain generation algorithm. At Studio B, they are asked to build a terrain system. Explain what the developer can and cannot legally bring to Studio B, referencing relevant IP law concepts.
questions.answer: The developer CANNOT bring: the source code (owned by Studio A under work-for-hire), any proprietary documentation or design specs, any assets or data from the project, or trade secrets such as specific algorithmic optimizations that are not publicly known. The developer CAN bring: their general knowledge and skills in procedural generation, understanding of publicly available algorithms and techniques (Perlin noise, diamond-square, etc.), experience with general tools and languages. The key distinction is between trade secrets and general knowledge. Under work-for-hire doctrine, the specific implementation belongs to Studio A. The developer should also check their employment agreement for non-compete clauses that might restrict working on similar technology for a specified period, and any invention assignment clauses that might claim ownership of related work done outside of employment.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies code and documentation as employer-owned under work-for-hire
Distinguishes between trade secrets and general professional knowledge
References relevant contract clauses (non-compete, invention assignment)
Practical advice is legally sound and professionally appropriate

major_assignments.title: Professional Portfolio and Career Strategy Package
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete professional package targeting a specific game industry role: a polished portfolio website with at least three case studies, an industry-targeted resume, a tailored cover letter for a real job posting, and a career strategy document mapping your five-year path.
objectives: Build a professional portfolio website with discipline-appropriate case studies
Write a resume and cover letter targeting a specific real-world job posting
Articulate a realistic career strategy informed by industry research
Demonstrate readiness for the game industry job search process
deliverables: Live portfolio website URL with at least 3 project case studies following the problem/process/solution/outcome format
One-page resume formatted for the target role, with ATS-compatible formatting
Cover letter for a specific current job posting (link to posting required)
Career strategy document (3-4 pages) mapping target roles, required skills, networking plan, and timeline
Peer review feedback from at least two classmates with documented revisions

criteria: Case studies follow the problem/process/solution/outcome format
Work samples are relevant to the target discipline and role
Visual presentation is professional and navigable
Site is functional across devices and loads quickly
Resume uses accomplishment-driven bullet points with quantified results
Cover letter is tailored to the specific posting and demonstrates research
Both documents are free of errors and professionally formatted
ATS compatibility verified through an online checker
Target roles are realistic and supported by industry research
Skills gap analysis identifies specific development areas
Networking plan includes concrete actions and timeline
Five-year path accounts for industry realities and multiple scenarios
Provided substantive feedback to at least two classmates
Documented revisions made based on received feedback
Engaged constructively in the review process
Feedback demonstrates critical evaluation skills
Complete professional package demonstrating job-search readiness
Portfolio case studies that clearly communicate skills and process
Career strategy grounded in industry research and self-assessment
Evidence of iteration through peer review process

major_assignments.title: Studio Simulation: Agile Game Production Sprint
major_assignments.description: Work in teams of 4-5 to produce a playable game prototype over five two-week sprints using full Agile/Scrum methodology. Teams must maintain a product backlog, run sprint ceremonies, track velocity, and produce sprint retrospective reports alongside the game deliverable.
objectives: Execute a multi-sprint Agile production process from concept to playable prototype
Practice Scrum ceremonies: sprint planning, daily standups, reviews, and retrospectives
Manage scope, priorities, and team dynamics in a realistic production environment
Produce professional production documentation alongside the game deliverable
deliverables: Playable game prototype delivered at the end of Sprint 5
Product backlog with full history of additions, completions, and reprioritizations
Sprint planning documents for all 5 sprints with estimated vs. actual velocity
Sprint retrospective reports for all 5 sprints documenting improvements
Individual contribution log and peer evaluation for each team member

criteria: All Scrum ceremonies conducted and documented for every sprint
Product backlog is maintained, prioritized, and refined throughout
Velocity tracking shows realistic estimation improving over sprints
Retrospective actions are implemented in subsequent sprints
Prototype is playable and demonstrates the core concept
Scope is appropriate for the team size and timeline
Quality reflects disciplined production practices
Feature completeness aligns with sprint commitments
Roles are clearly defined and responsibilities are shared equitably
Communication is documented and professional
Conflicts are addressed constructively through retrospectives
Peer evaluations reflect genuine assessment of contributions
Documentation is thorough, consistent, and professional
Sprint reports capture meaningful data and actionable insights
Individual contribution logs are specific and verifiable
Overall documentation tells the story of the production process
Full Agile production process executed with authentic ceremonies and artifacts
Playable prototype reflecting disciplined scope management
Effective team collaboration with documented conflict resolution
Professional production documentation suitable for a studio environment

quizzes.description: Assessment of industry knowledge, professional practices, and legal concepts

portfolio_package.description: Professional portfolio, resume, cover letter, and career strategy deliverables

studio_simulation.description: Team-based Agile production sprint with game prototype and full process documentation

class_participation.description: Engagement in mock interviews, portfolio critiques, guest speaker Q&A, and class discussions

professional_development_journal.description: Weekly reflections on industry research, networking activities, and skill development

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all guest speaker sessions, mock interviews, and team sprint ceremonies. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late individual assignments are penalized 10% per day. Sprint deliverables cannot be submitted late as they depend on team cadence. Extensions require advance arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Portfolio and career strategy assignments are individual work. The studio simulation is a required team project with peer evaluation contributing to individual grades.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All portfolio work must be your own or properly attributed. Resume content must be truthful. Fabricating experience, credentials, or project contributions will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to a project management tool (Trello or Jira free tier), web hosting for portfolio (GitHub Pages, Netlify, or similar free service), and their preferred game development tools for the studio simulation.
